text: I've been tasked to discuss laparoscopic left hepatectomy. So I have no disclosures. This is the outline of my presentation. I give you a brief introduction. We talk about patient port positioning, mobilization of the left liver, key steps in the ultrasound, the portal dissection, the chymotranssection, how we divide the left hepatic vein, and I give you some important points at the end.

So if we look at this data here from our friend Ruben, then we find that the left hepatectomy is actually less common than, I would say, a more complicated right hepatectomy. It's the fifth most performed liver resection. And if you look at the data comparing lab versus open for major resection in this recent study, we see that the postoperative outcomes seem favorable.

One word of caution in this session that is very technical. In the Western world, I think a left hepatectomy should be an uncommon procedure. If you look at colorectal liver metastasis that are located in the left liver, and we look at the group of patients that were resected with a parenchymal sparing versus non-parenchymal sparing approach. There's no difference actually in outcome the first time around. But colorectal liver metastasis is a chronic disease and three out of four patients will recur. And the ability to rescue this recurrence goes away if you're wasteful with the left liver. So you can see here the data for month after the hepatectomy for the parenchymal sparing approach versus the non-parenchymal sparing approach.

The patient is positioned in the French position for a laparoscopic left hepatectomy, with the surgeon standing between the patient's legs. The first port is placed midway between the rib cage and the umbilicus, ensuring proximity to the liver fulcrum for optimal access.
The surgical setup is prepared for portal dissection, followed by the parenchymal transection and division of the left hepatic vein.

The surgeon begins mobilization of the liver by taking down the falciform ligament, with the option to leave it attached for guidance during parenchymal transection. The left triangular and coronary ligaments are also dissected. A visible metastasis is centered over the middle hepatic vein, extending into the right liver segments eight and five. An ultrasound is used to assist in the laparoscopic left hepatectomy.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to examine the liver, focusing on the middle hepatic vein from the left side of the falciform ligament. The probe is positioned under the falciform ligament to visualize the area and exclude any metastasis in the future liver remnant.
The surgeon exposes the middle hepatic vein, revealing its branches draining into it, as outlined by the ultrasound imaging.

I always teach the fellows that the middle hepatic vein is the friend of the liver surgeon. It will guide you through the parenchymal transection. Once you know where the middle pedic vein is, you cannot really get lost during the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon performs portal dissection, carefully navigating the portal structures with awareness of the type three bile duct anatomy to avoid devascularizing the right posterior portal pedicle while dividing the left bile duct.
So you want to be cognizant of that anatomy and avoid this division. So you need to study your preoperative imaging very carefully to not have any surprises.
The surgeon has removed the gallbladder, which is often left attached as a handle during liver resection. The cystic duct is disconnected to lower the hilar plate.

The surgeon dissects the hepatic artery, specifically the left hepatic artery, using an intraglyconian approach towards the portal confluence. The beginning of the right portal vein and the left portal vein are visible, with careful exposure to ensure the confluence is not narrowed.
The surgeon divides the portal vein, aiming to avoid remnant liver ischemia by preserving perfusion to the caudate lobe branches, facilitating later exposure of the bile ducts.
The surgeon exposes the area where the left hepatic vein drains into the inferior vena cava (IVC), with the left liver positioned superiorly and the caudate lobe visible inferiorly.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection on the devascularized left liver, using ultrasound guidance to assist in the precise division of liver tissue.

The surgeon identifies the middle hepatic vein, which serves as a guide during the parenchymal transection. The vein is located approximately 1.8 centimeters from the biliary confluence, providing a visual reference for the ongoing surgical procedure.

The surgeon performs a parenchymal transection of the liver, starting from the less risky peripheral area and progressing towards the more risky central area. The transection follows a convex peripheral to central approach, minimizing blood pooling within the transection plane. The middle hepatic vein serves as a guide for the central portion of the transection.

The surgeon begins the parenchymal transection with a view of the middle hepatic vein towards the left, near the biliary confluence. As the transection progresses, the surgeon follows the middle hepatic vein, identifying and counting the draining veins such as V4B ventral and dorsal, and V4A drainage vein to minimize bleeding. The view is prepared for staple division using an open book approach.

The surgeon performs liver transection on a chemo-damaged liver using the crush clamp technique with bipolar forceps. The tumor involves the middle hepatic vein, preventing its use as a guide. The bile duct is divided, causing increased bleeding due to centripetal flow, which indicates proximity to the bile duct and facilitates the opening of the parenchymal transection.

The surgeon identifies the V-five drainage vein on the right side of the liver, using it as a guide towards the middle hepatic vein during the parenchymal transection. This approach is adapted due to the metastasis involving the middle hepatic vein.

The completion of the parenchymal transection reveals the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the right hepatic vein. The surgeon prepares to divide the left hepatic vein, with a fellow holding a vascular clamp for potential stapler misfiring. Sutures are used to facilitate the stapling process, and the specimen is removed through a Pfannenstiel incision.

Okay, so we talked today about how to do a left hepatectomy, and I think that the left lobectomy for metastasis should be a rare procedure in order to preserve parenchyma. It is important to define the hyla in middle hepatic vein anatomy prior to surgery. and it is key to use intraoperative ultrasound to guide your parenchymal transection and also for identification of all the metastases. The middle hepatic vein should guide the parenchymal transection and a peripheral to central dissection approach helps you to expose the transection plan as you're moving through the liver. I would like to thank my team for their support and you for your attention. Thank you very much.
